articles.title: Not in a Celebratory Mood? America Has Overcome Hard Times Before, and These 5 Historical Facts Prove It
articles.content:
这则新闻传遍了大街小巷：总统被指控挪用联邦资金用于个人项目。国会成员收受贿赂。敌对党派分化选民，然后提交来自同一州的多个相互矛盾的选举结果。佛罗里达不出所料地处于这场混乱的中心。骚乱爆发。整个争端最终被交给一个特别委员会处理，而选举结束数月后这个国家都没有新总统。听起来很熟悉？这场选举乱局并非来自2020年。做好心理准备：我们说的是1876年。“这无疑是我们经历过的最腐败的总统选举，”历史学家格雷格·杰克逊说道，他是犹他谷大学副教授，也是热门播客“不会无聊的历史”的主持人。“而且其腐败程度让其他所有选举都相形见绌。”

杰克逊确实有发言权。他花了三年时间深入研究美国历史，为新书《都经历过：我们的历史如何证明我们能克服一切》做调研，该书恰在美国250岁生日时出版——说实话，这个里程碑并没有让该国大部分人真正欢呼雀跃。就在我采访杰克逊的同一天，我还采访了一位101岁的老人。当我问她在当前局势下会对那些感到紧张的人说些什么时，我期待她会说些安慰的话。相反，她告诉我这是“这个国家有史以来最糟糕的时期”，人们有理由感到恐惧。这是一个经历过二战、大萧条和民权运动的女人。当我把与老人的对话告诉杰克逊时，他毫不犹豫。“哦，托马斯·杰斐逊也说过同样的话，”他说。在1790年代极端党派的印刷战之后——顺便说一下，这场战争是杰斐逊自己引发的——他曾悲叹国会议员们过去会在政策上持不同意见，然后仍然一起共进晚餐。而现在，他写道，他们过街都会避开彼此。“为什么人们如此残忍？为什么社会如此不文明？为什么？？？”他大声疾呼。（我是在转述。）

“就连托马斯·杰斐逊——主要起草《独立宣言》的人、人类历史上最杰出的头脑之一——也陷入了同样的思维习惯：浪漫化过去，同时非常强烈地感受到当下的痛苦和困境，这既可笑又令人安慰，”杰克逊说。

但这种怀疑态度虽然可以理解，却可能是错误的。事实上，这正是杰克逊书中的核心前提：今天发生的任何事情都不是前所未有的，我们不仅经历过更糟糕的时期，而且实际上已经更强大、更完善地走出了困境。这是杰克逊带着他的现场表演和播客在30个州巡演时一直在传递的信息，该播客已被下载数千万次。每到一站，他都会听到我那位百岁朋友的回声：人们感到绝望。他们觉得事情从未如此糟糕。所以如果你也对当今美国的状况感觉不太好，这不只是你。从历史上看，也从来不是只有你。但当时有充分的理由保持乐观，现在我们有同样多甚至更多的理由对我们的国家保持乐观。为了向你证明这一点，我请杰克逊带我们回顾美国历史上的五个与当今情况相似的故事。继续阅读，了解当前政治气候的背景，更好地理解我们能从哪里出发。订阅《读者文摘》的Read Up通讯，获取更多历史、幽默、清洁、旅行、科技和有趣的事实。

如果你看过《汉密尔顿》，就会知道开国元勋们不需要社交媒体就能进行diss track大战。在社交媒体、有线新闻、算法出现之前，亚历山大·汉密尔顿和托马斯·杰斐逊就曾在公开场合用各种阴险手段试图毁掉对方。“我可以给你讲1790年代发生的类似故事，”杰克逊说，“亚历山大·汉密尔顿和托马斯·杰斐逊在媒体上竭尽全力诋毁对方——使用笔名，基本上就像今天有人在社交媒体上使用机器人或虚假账号来掩盖他们说的话。”

杰克逊在书第一章中记录的这场印刷战不仅仅是关于党派偏见。本杰明·富兰克林本人——开国元勋、政治家、在雷暴中放风筝的人——曾印刷了一份精心制作的、完全虚构的恐怖故事，以在与英国谈判中获取优势。他伪造了一整份真实波士顿报纸的假版本，编造了关于美洲原住民剥头皮杀害数百名妇女、儿童和婴儿的报道——他认为这些可怕的细节足以震惊英国公众，让他们支持美国要求赔偿的要求。“这个故事是可怕的，而且是纯粹的虚构，”杰克逊说。“这是一个失误，本杰明·富兰克林知道这一点。他放手那份报纸的那一刻就感觉到了。”

教训不是我们的开国元勋是伪君子（虽然可能有一点）。而是 misinformation 并不是互联网时代的产物。这是人类的产物。“我们一直都有假新闻，”杰克逊说，并指出我们有能力应对它。“公民和共和国有责任思考、学习、研究。如果没有人民做大量工作，就不会有人民自治的政府。”

如果1月6日让你觉得美国的政治暴力是新出现的、失控的，请想想1856年。马萨诸塞州参议员查尔斯·萨姆纳发表了一篇激烈演讲，谴责奴隶制，并以名字嘲讽了一位南卡罗来纳州参议员。两天后，那位参议员的表弟、众议员普雷斯顿·布鲁克斯走进参议院大厅，用一根金属头的手杖残忍地殴打萨姆纳，导致萨姆纳三年无法返回工作。南方将布鲁克斯视为英雄。仰慕者送给他纪念手杖。北方感到震惊。这个国家感觉正在被撕裂。听起来很熟悉？杰克逊认为应该如此。政治暴力——包括在政府大厅内发生的那种——一直是美国生活的反复出现的特征，而不是一种异常。改变的不是人性。最终改变的是足够多的美国人拒绝接受它。杰克逊认为，1876年的海斯-蒂尔登选举是特别重要的一章。“等你看完这次选举的调查，对被质疑结果的调查产生了证据——想想看——至少有数百名黑人美国人被杀。”当时，白人至上主义准军事组织正在谋杀黑人选民以压制共和党选票，而且这很有效。仅在南卡罗来纳州，估计就有150名黑人共和党人被杀害。如果南方没有选民压制，海斯很可能就赢了。然后还有记录在案的贿赂。“我们有收据，用21世纪的流行语来说19世纪的选举，”杰克逊说。当选官员被提供现金以换取改变投票。国会议员们把钱带到有争议的南方各州，希望亲自行贿。四个州提交了多套相互矛盾的选举报告。佛罗里达州独自就提交了三份。国会最终把整个烂摊子丢给了一个由15人组成的特别委员会，委员会按严格的党派路线投票。获胜者在选举日四个月后的二月才被决定。“在2020年，我们对选举结果有一些质疑，”杰克逊说。“但当国会面临不止一套选举结果时，情况要丑陋得多。这甚至不是一个是与非的问题——我们是否要计算这个州的票？而是我们要计算这个州的哪一套票？”

杰克逊喜欢说，历史不会重演，但常常押韵（这是马克·吐温的名言）。杰克逊在书中讲述的最痛苦的故事之一不是关于选举或骚乱。而是关于几乎发生了什么……然后没有发生什么。内战之后，在重建时期，黑人美国人短暂地获得了投票权、担任公职的机会，以及美国的承诺。然后，在杰克逊称之为美国历史上最具影响力的失败之一的时刻，这个国家在1870年代开始抛弃他们，任由吉姆·克劳法肆虐。“这绝不是一个快乐的故事，”杰克逊承认。“我们如此接近于真正将黑人美国人带入美国实验的承诺中，然后最终我们抛弃了他们，让他们只能得到一半的措施。比奴隶制好，但仅仅好一点。我们离开了近一个世纪。”

但即使在这里，杰克逊仍然坚持更完整的画面。因为在重建运动中——以及在其丑陋的瓦解中（其中包括真正的武装起义来推翻南方的民选政府）——都有英雄的身影。那些挺身而出的人们。尽管面临暴力和恐吓仍行使权利的选民。坚持到底的立法者。拒绝简单服从的普通公民。“没有低谷就没有英雄，”他说。“即使在我们进入这些黑暗时刻时，我也在指出美国人可以选择参与的地方。”

如果人工智能让你感觉是对真理和民主的前所未有的威胁，杰克逊有一些有用的历史背景。1890年代，蒸汽动力印刷机的引入——每小时能生产数千份报纸而不是每天数百份——创造了一个在当时人们看来同样混乱和危险的媒体环境。约瑟夫·普利策和威廉·兰道夫·赫斯特利用新技术开创了后来被称为“黄色新闻”的报道方式：煽动性的、往往是捏造的故事，旨在激怒读者和推销报纸。一些历史学家甚至认为他们的报道帮助将国家推入了美西战争。“这绝对是一场革命，毫无疑问，”杰克逊在谈到今天的AI时说。“但我们经常忘记的是，以往的这些革命之后总会有一段时间的调整。公众会搞清楚。我们学会如何在这个新的信息获取水平上运作。”

换句话说：印刷机在1890年代看起来是灾难性的。收音机在1930年代看起来是灾难性的。电视在1960年代看起来是灾难性的。每一次，美国人最终都适应了。“我们从用羽毛笔书写到用拇指滑动，”杰克逊说，“我们应对了每一次颠覆、每一次大规模的工业革命、每一次技术革命，不胜枚举。我觉得我们会再次做到这一点。”

这是我最深入追问杰克逊的问题，而他的回答很细致。作为历史学家的本能是找到押韵之处。他提到读到一篇关于当前政府被指控挪用国家公园管理局资金用于白宫改善的文章——并立即将其与他书中的第三章联系起来，在那一章中，约翰·昆西·亚当斯被指控使用公共资金将白宫变成“赌场”。（他的罪过？买了一个棋盘和一张台球桌。当时的愤怒程度大致相当于今天你在推特上可能看到的。）

“我不是在为这项指控开脱，”杰克逊谨慎地说。“但美国人民思考、质疑、怀疑总统是否在享受特权？这是历史在押韵。”

今天发生的事情中有一些是真正前所未有的吗？当然有，但他的担忧并不是关于任何特定行为是否前所未有。而是关于美国人是否足够关心去回应。“真正的问题不是它是否真正前所未有，而是美国人有多关心——我们真的要反击，还是我们要耸肩？”杰克逊说。“因为如果我们耸肩，我们基本上就是在给所有腐败的政客敲响晚餐铃。我们正在向我们的公仆发出他们能逃脱什么的信号。”

他顿了顿，然后补充道：“对这个国家来说，没有比犬儒主义更大的威胁了。”

杰克逊不想仅仅让你感觉更好。他想让你有所行动。在他的书结尾，他大量借鉴了开国元勋的“公共美德”概念——即民主需要积极投入的公民才能生存的理念。以下是他说的如何实践它的方法。

1. 成为一个终身学习者——但要选好你的领域。了解你关心的问题。“不要感到畏惧，觉得你必须知道一切，”杰克逊说。“选一件你感兴趣的事。”



深入了解两三个问题，这些就成为你的试金石。当一位政治家开始高谈阔论某个议题时——比如关税到底是不是税——你就心中有数了。然后你就会知道是否可以信任他们。”

2. 超越选举日积极参与。“11月去投票就像1月份办理健身房会员卡，”杰克逊说，“你感觉良好，有了会员卡，你可以说服自己你去了健身房，虽然已经好几个月没去了。但如果你想要更好的候选人，你就必须参与筛选过程——党团会议、初选、地方选举。那才是我们真正决定谁会出现在选票上的环节。那就是为什么我们不会在11月面对'怎么又是两个烂苹果'的困境。”

3. 管理你自己的信息摄入。“说互联网上全是假新闻的人，就像在说'快餐店太多了，我怎么才能吃得健康？'好吧，你从它们旁边开过去就行了，去超市买食材。没人逼你每顿都吃薯条和奶昔。人们对算法感到恐惧，我理解。但归根结底，我们自己决定消费什么。你从未有过如此便捷地获取优质信息的渠道。别再用信息焦虑刷屏来获取新闻了。管理好你自己的信息源。关注权威媒体，包括那些你不同意的观点。”

他自己的阅读清单：《外交事务》提供国家安全背景，《华盛顿邮报》和《纽约时报》提供左倾的权威报道，《华尔街日报》提供右倾视角。另外，还有他订阅的当地报纸——他认为这是必不可少的。“那才是遏制腐败的利器，”他说，“当市长知道一个县里600人都将知道发生了什么，这就能让市长保持警醒。”

但也许加强公共美德最好的方式，是回顾我们历史上那些平凡的英雄，然后决定成为他们那样的人。当我问杰克逊书中最喜欢的人物时，他毫不犹豫：威廉·巴尼，1812年巴尔的摩骚乱期间的一位民兵指挥官。1812年的巴尔的摩是一个火药桶。一群杰斐逊派共和党人（不是今天的共和党——而是杰斐逊和麦迪逊的政党）正以可怕的暴力手段攻击联邦党对手。暴民们弄到了一门大炮。威廉·巴尼是共和党人，联邦党人是他的政敌。但当暴民们把大炮对准联邦党人群准备开火时，巴尼走上前去，用胸膛抵住了炮口。他站在大炮和目标之间，愿意为阻止自己这一方杀害政见不同者而死。“他没有退让，”杰克逊叙述道，“拿着火柴的人正在靠近大炮，显然他们要点燃引信——但最终勉强避免了。然而有一个人准备好为他的党友——不，是为那些与他政见不同、正在被他盟友威胁的美国同胞——赴死。”

杰克逊停顿了一下。“如果那些人当时存在，这些人就依然存在。我们可以找到他们。我们可以成为他们。”

有许多方式可以参与政治进程，尤其是在地方层面，杰克逊指出正是在那里你可以产生最大的影响。他本人在帮助地方选举方面做志愿者。在纳什维尔的巡回售书活动中，有人问杰克逊是否考虑过竞选总统。“这让我开怀大笑，”他说，“我都有点不好意思承认，这确实是最让我意外的问题。”

但他会考虑竞选公职吗？（在与他聊了一个小时后，我会投他的票。他就是这么聪明。）“我认为这取决于一个人在什么地方最有用，”杰克逊谦逊地说，“我无法想象我现在担任公职会比过去几年写这本书为国家带来更多益处。”

对于一个此刻亟需更多自我信心的国家来说，这或许恰恰是正确的答案。“我曾凝视我们历史上最深的黑暗，”杰克逊说，“我审视了我们最低谷的时刻。而这一切只是向我证明了这个国家、这个民族的韧性。我相信这种韧性今天依然存在。”
articles.summary:
It’s all over the news: The president stands accused of raiding federal funds for personal projects. Members of Congress are caught paying and accepting bribes. Rival parties divide voters, then submit multiple conflicting sets of election results from the same state. Florida, predictably, is at the center of the mess. There are riots. The whole dispute ultimately gets handed to a special commission to sort out, and the country doesn’t have a new president for months after the election.

Sound familiar? This tale of electoral chaos isn’t from 2020. Brace yourself: We’re talking about 1876.

“It is unquestionably the most corrupt presidential election we have ever had,” says historian Greg Jackson, an associate history professor at Utah Valley University and the host of the wildly popular podcast History That Doesn’t Suck. “And by a margin that makes everything else pale.”

Jackson would know. He spent three years deep-diving into American history for his new book, Been There, Done That: How Our History Shows What We Can Overcome, published just in time for America’s 250th birthday—a milestone that, let’s be honest, a good portion of the country isn’t exactly throwing confetti about.

Why cynicism about our country is on the rise

The same day I interviewed Jackson, I interviewed a woman who is 101 years old. When I asked what she’d say to people who are nervous about these times, I expected her to say something reassuring. Instead, she told me this was “hands down the worst this country has ever been” and that people were right to be scared. This is a woman who lived through World War II, the Great Depression and the Civil Rights Movement.

When I told Jackson about my conversation with the woman, he didn’t miss a beat. “Oh, Thomas Jefferson said the same thing,” he said. In the years after the hyperpartisan print wars of the 1790s—wars Jefferson himself helped ignite, by the way—he dramatically lamented that men in Congress used to disagree on policy and then still go to dinner together. Now, he wrote, they crossed the street to avoid each other. “Why are people so cruel? Why is society so uncivil? Whyyyy?” he cried. (I’m paraphrasing.)

“It’s both hilarious and reassuring,” Jackson says, “that even Thomas Jefferson—primarily the author of the Declaration of Independence, one of the most brilliant minds in human history—fell into that same human habit of romanticizing the past while feeling, very viscerally, all the pains and woes of the present.”

But this cynicism, while understandable, may be misplaced. In fact, that is the whole premise of Jackson’s book: Nothing happening today is unprecedented, and not only have we been through worse, but we’ve actually come through it stronger and better. That’s the message Jackson has been carrying to audiences across 30 states with his live show and podcast, which has been downloaded tens of millions of times. At every stop, he hears echoes of my centenarian friend: People are despondent. They feel like things have never been worse.

So if you, too, aren’t feeling great about the state of things in America today, it’s not just you. And historically speaking, it never was.

But there were many reasons to be optimistic then, and there are just as many, if not more, reasons to be optimistic about our country now. To prove that to you, I asked Jackson to walk us through five stories in American history that parallel what is happening today. Read on to put the current political climate into perspective and better understand where we can go from here.

Get Reader’s Digest’s Read Up newsletter for more history, humor, cleaning, travel, tech and fun facts all week long.

Misinformation started with the Founding Fathers

If you’ve seen Hamilton, then you know that the Founding Fathers didn’t need social media to get into a battle of diss tracks. Before social media, before cable news, before algorithms, Alexander Hamilton and Thomas Jefferson tried to destroy each other in public, often underhanded ways. “I can regale you with tales of this happening in the 1790s,” Jackson says, “with Alexander Hamilton and Thomas Jefferson doing their best to kill each other in the press—under pseudonyms, basically like someone taking social media today and using bots or fake handles to cover the tracks of what they’re saying.”

The print war Jackson chronicles in Chapter 1 of his book wasn’t just about partisan spin. Benjamin Franklin himself—Founding Father, statesman, flier of kites in lightning storms—printed an elaborate, completely fabricated horror story to gain leverage in negotiations with Britain. He forged an entire fake edition of a real Boston newspaper, complete with invented accounts of Native Americans scalping hundreds of women, children and infants—gruesome enough, he hoped, to shock the British public into supporting American demands for reparations. “The story was horrific, and it was pure fiction,” Jackson says. “It was a blunder, and Ben Franklin knew it. He felt it in his soul the moment he let go of the paper.”

The lesson isn’t that our Founders were hypocrites (though maybe a little). It’s that misinformation isn’t a product of the internet age. It’s a product of being human. “We’ve had fake news constantly,” Jackson says, noting that we are equipped to deal with it. “It is incumbent upon citizens and the republic to think, to learn, to study. You don’t get government by the people without the people doing a lot of work.”

Violence has always been a part of our politics, and things were really bad in the 1800s

If January 6th made you feel like political violence in America was something new and out of control, consider 1856. Senator Charles Sumner of Massachusetts gave a fiery speech denouncing slavery and mocked a South Carolina senator by name. Two days later, that senator’s cousin, Representative Preston Brooks, walked onto the Senate floor and beat Sumner so savagely with a metal-tipped cane that Sumner couldn’t return to work for three years.

The South celebrated Brooks as a hero. Admirers sent him commemorative canes. The North was horrified. The country felt like it was being torn apart.

Sound familiar? Jackson thinks it should. Political violence—including the kind that happens inside the halls of government itself—has been a recurring feature of American life, not an aberration. What changed wasn’t human nature. What changed, eventually, was enough Americans refusing to accept it.

Contested elections are as American as apple pie

The Hayes-Tilden election of 1876 is a particularly important chapter, according to Jackson. “By the time you get through this election, the investigation of the challenged results produced evidence of—and think about this—somewhere in at least the low hundreds, Black Americans being killed.” At the time, White supremacist paramilitary groups were murdering Black voters to suppress the Republican vote, and it worked. In South Carolina alone, an estimated 150 Black Republicans were killed. Had there not been voter suppression in the South, Hayes likely would have won.

And then there was the documented bribery. “We have the receipts, to use the 21st-century phrase on a 19th-century election,” Jackson says. Elected officials were offered cash to change their votes. Members of Congress literally carried money to contested Southern states, hoping to pay out bribes in person. Four states submitted multiple conflicting sets of electoral returns. Florida alone sent three. Congress ultimately punted the whole mess to a special 15-person commission, which voted along strict party lines. The winner was decided in February, four months after Election Day.

“In 2020, we had some questioning of the returns,” Jackson says. “But it’s so much uglier when Congress is facing more than one set of returns. This isn’t even a yes-or-no, are we going to count the state? It’s which set are we going to count from this state?”

History doesn’t repeat itself, Jackson likes to say, quoting Mark Twain. But it often rhymes.

We have abandoned our ideals before—and then reclaimed them

One of the most painful stories in Jackson’s book isn’t about an election or a riot. It’s about what almost happened … and then didn’t. After the Civil War, during Reconstruction, Black Americans briefly gained access to the ballot, to office, to the promises of America. Then, in a moment Jackson calls one of the most consequential failures in U.S. history, the country abandoned them to the Jim Crow laws, starting in the 1870s.

“This is not a happy story by any means,” Jackson acknowledges. “We came so close to truly bringing Black Americans into the promises of the American experiment, and then ultimately we abandoned them to this half measure. Better than slavery, but only just. And we walked away for nearly a century.”

But even here, Jackson insists on the fuller picture. Because embedded in Reconstruction—and in its ugly dismantling, which included real armed insurrections to overthrow elected governments in the South—are the heroes. The people who showed up. Voters who exercised their rights, despite violence and intimidation. Legislators who held the line as long as they could. Ordinary citizens who refused to simply comply.

“You don’t have heroes without the lows,” he says. “Even as we go into these dark moments, I’m pointing out where Americans can choose to engage.”

New tech has always disrupted democracy, and we’ve always adapted

If AI feels like an unprecedented threat to truth and democracy, Jackson has some useful historical context. In the 1890s, the introduction of the steam-powered printing press—which could crank out thousands of papers per hour instead of hundreds per day—created a media environment that felt just as chaotic and dangerous to people at the time.

Joseph Pulitzer and William Randolph Hearst used the new technology to pioneer what became known as yellow journalism: sensationalized, often fabricated stories designed to inflame readers and sell papers. Some historians even believe their coverage helped push the country into the Spanish-American War.

“This is an absolute revolution, no question,” Jackson says of AI today. “But what we often forget about our previous revolutions is that those disruptions are always followed by an adjustment. The public figures it out. We learn how to operate with this new level of access to information.”

In other words: The printing press felt apocalyptic in the 1890s. Radio felt apocalyptic in the 1930s. Television felt apocalyptic in the 1960s. Each time, Americans eventually adapted.

“We’ve gone from writing with a quill to swiping with our thumbs,” Jackson says, “and we’ve managed every disruption, every massive industrial revolution, every technological revolution, you name it. I feel like we’re gonna do that again.”

Is anything actually unprecedented right now?

This is the question I pushed Jackson on the hardest, and his answer was nuanced. His instinct, as a historian, is to find the rhyme. He mentions reading a recent article about the current administration allegedly redirecting National Park Service funds for White House improvements—and immediately connects it to Chapter 3 of his book, where John Quincy Adams was accused of using public money to turn the White House into a “gambling den.” (His sin? Buying a chess board and a pool table. The outrage was roughly equivalent to what you might see on Twitter today.)

“I’m not absolving the accusation,” Jackson is careful to say. “But the American people thinking, questioning, wondering, doubting whether the president is taking privileges? It’s history rhyming.”

Are there things happening today that have genuinely never happened before? Certainly, but his concern isn’t really about whether any given action is unprecedented. It’s about whether Americans care enough to respond.

“The real question is less about whether it’s truly unprecedented and more about how much Americans care—are we going to actually push back, or are we going to shrug?” Jackson says. “Because if we shrug, we are basically ringing the dinner bell to all corrupt politicians. We are signaling to our public servants what they can get away with.”

He pauses, then adds: “There is no greater threat to this nation than cynicism.”

What to do with this info

Jackson doesn’t want you just to feel better. He wants you to do something. In his book’s conclusion, he leans heavily on the Founders’ concept of “public virtue”—the idea that a democracy requires active, engaged citizens to survive. Here’s how he says to practice it.

1. Become a perpetual learner—but pick your lane. Learn about the issues that concern you. “Don’t get intimidated and feel like you have to know everything,” Jackson says. “Pick a thing you’re into. Get a decent grasp of two or three issues, and those become your litmus test. When a politician starts sounding off on whatever it might be—are tariffs really taxes, or aren’t they?—you’ll know. And then you’ll know whether you can trust them.”

2. Show up beyond Election Day. “Voting in November is like getting a gym membership in January,” Jackson says. “You feel good, you’ve got your membership card, you can convince yourself you go to the gym even though it’s been months. But if you want better candidates, you have to be in the vetting process—the caucuses, the primaries, the local races. That’s where we actually pick who’s on the ballot. That’s how we stop ending up in November going, ‘How did we end up with a choice between Tweedledee and Tweedledumb?'”

3. Curate your own information diet. “People saying the internet is only full of fake news is like saying ‘There are so many fast-food joints, how can I eat healthy?’ Well, you drive past them. You go to the grocery store. No one is forcing you to get french fries and shakes for every meal. People are terrified of algorithms, and I understand that. But at the end of the day, we decide what we consume,” he says. “You have never had more ready access to better information. Stop using doomscrolling for your news. Curate your own. Follow reputable sources, including ones you disagree with.”

His own reading list: Foreign Affairs for national-security context, the Washington Post and New York Times for left-leaning reputable coverage, and the Wall Street Journal for the right. Plus, his local newspaper—which he subscribes to and considers essential. “That’s what checks corruption,” he says. “When the mayor knows everyone in a county of 600 will hear what happened, it keeps that mayor on his or her toes.”

Meet William Barney, the most underrated hero in American history

But perhaps the best way to strengthen public virtue is to look at all of the small heroes throughout our history and decide to be like them. When I asked Jackson for his favorite character in the book, he didn’t hesitate: William Barney, a militia commander during the Baltimore Riots of 1812.

Baltimore in 1812 was a powder keg. A mob of Jeffersonian Republicans (not the modern GOP—the party of Jefferson and Madison) was turning on Federalist opponents with terrifying violence. The mob had gotten its hands on a cannon.

William Barney was a Republican. The Federalists were his political enemies. But when the mob moved toward the cannon to fire it into the crowd of Federalists, Barney walked forward and pressed his chest against it. He stood between the cannon and its target, willing to die to stop his own side from killing people he disagreed with.

“He doesn’t move,” Jackson recounts. “The man with the match is moving toward the cannon, it’s clear they are going to light it—and it is barely avoided. But here’s a man who’s ready to die not just for his fellow partisans but for his fellow Americans with whom he disagrees, who are being threatened by those with whom he does agree.”

Jackson pauses.

“If they existed then, those people still exist now. We can find them. We can be them.”

Be the change you want to see

There are many ways you can participate in the political process, especially at the local level, and Jackson points out that is where you can have the biggest effect. He himself volunteers helping in local elections.

At the end of a book-tour stop in Nashville, someone asked Jackson if he’d ever consider running for president. “That made me laugh heartily,” he says. “I’m mildly embarrassed to even admit it, and it definitely caught me most off guard.”

But would he consider running for office? (After spending the better part of an hour chatting with him, I would vote for him. He’s that smart.) “I think that’s a question of where someone is most useful,” Jackson demurs. “I can’t fathom that I would be doing more good for the country holding office right now than having written this book over the last few years.”

For a country that could use a little more faith in itself right now, that might be exactly right.

“I’ve stared into the deepest darkness of our history,” Jackson says. “I’ve looked at our absolute lowest moments. And it has done nothing but show me the resilience of this nation, of this people. And I believe that resilience is still here today.”

About the expert

  • Greg Jackson is an associate professor of history at Utah Valley University, where he serves as the America 250 professor in the Center for Constitutional Studies and is a senior fellow in the Center for National Security Studies. He is the creator, host and head writer of History That Doesn’t Suck, one of the top history podcasts on Apple and Spotify. His new book, Been There, Done That: How Our History Shows What We Can Overcome, is out now.

Why trust us

At Reader’s Digest, we’re committed to producing high-quality content by writers with expertise and experience in their field in consultation with relevant, qualified experts. We rely on reputable primary sources, including government and professional organizations and academic institutions as well as our writers’ personal experiences where appropriate. We verify all facts and data, back them with credible sourcing and revisit them over time to ensure they remain accurate and up to date. Read more about our team, our contributors and our editorial policies.

articles.title: I Tried “Microbreaks” to Be More Productive—And They Changed My Day in Ways I Didn’t Expect
articles.content:
周一下午两点四十三分，我已经和椅子融为一体了。不是那种平和的、复合人体工学原理的融合。更像是“有人见过她眨眼吗”那种程度的融合。我回了邮件，改了文案，同一个标签页打开了三次，水瓶被我彻底无视到它简直成了装饰品。我的身体技术上还在场，但我的大脑已经开始给我放旋转彩虹轮了。这就是工作日中我通常会做出两个糟糕选择的时刻。要么我继续硬撑，称之为自律；要么我进行我坚称是“快速休息”的休息，然后不知怎么就一头扎进某个Reddit帖子45分钟，琢磨着怎么重返正常社会，更别说我的收件箱了。

所以当我听说微休息——那些短暂的、有计划的停顿，据说能帮助你通过短暂地不工作来更好地工作——我以一种只有疯狂赶截止日期的人才能有的怀疑态度看待它。想法很美好。非常可爱。但当我现在的选择似乎是“永不停歇”或“不小心消失在手机里”时，三分钟的休息真的有用吗？不过这个承诺确实很诱人：减少僵硬、减少脑雾，也许还能提高专注力。而且这个想法背后有大量研究支持。2022年发表在《公共科学图书馆·综合》期刊上的一篇综述发现，10分钟或更短的短暂休息可以帮助减少疲劳并改善身心健康。哥伦比亚大学研究人员2023年的一项研究发现，每半小时进行五分钟的步行休息可以帮助抵消久坐带来的一些身体影响。

这足以让我尝试微休息一周。我并没有指望离开办公桌几分钟能给我的的一天带来任何有意义的变化。但我通常的做法——一直坐着直到大脑变得模糊、肩膀耸到耳朵的位置——显然不是一个成功的策略。所以尝试一下也没什么坏处。

然而，我发现的东西让我重新思考自己对工作和生活的整个方法。

简而言之，是的。我们中的许多人长时间坐在办公桌前而没有有意义的休息——甚至午餐时间也未能幸免于笔记本电脑的入侵。根据ezCater 2024年午餐报告，98%的员工认识到午餐休息可以帮助工作表现和生产力，但只有38%的人每天午餐时间会离开办公桌休息。Opinium Research为Little Leaf Farms进行的另一项2024年调查显示，76%的非远程企业员工至少有一半时间在办公桌前吃午餐。在办公桌吃午餐是那种在用一只手拿着叉子回邮件、键盘上沾满沙拉酱之前感觉高效的习惯之一。

但问题也比午餐更大。我们坐下来，锁定目标，然后期望我们的大脑像不需要运动、不需要日光、不需要偶尔看看屏幕以外的东西的机器一样运转。大卫·斯特雷尔博士说，问题不仅仅是整天坐着让我们僵硬。问题在于我们的大脑并不是为长时间不休息地持续专注于高要求工作而构建的。随着时间的推移，表现会开始下降，研究人员将这种模式称为“警觉性衰减”。“关于这方面的文献很多，”斯特雷尔说。“当人们需要长时间保持任务时，最初表现会从较高水平下降，然后他们的表现会持续退化。”

微休息是工作日中短暂的、有意的停顿，通常只持续几分钟，最多不超过10分钟。这听起来小得几乎可以忽略不计，这可能就是为什么我最初低估了它们。我听到“休息”这个词时会想象更实质性的东西：午餐、小憩、两周的静修，期间没有人能让我“回过头来讨论”。但微休息更像是标点符号。它们在工作日变成一个没有逗号、腰痛缠身的漫长句子之前打断它。关键不是把另一项任务塞进去；而是停止做主要任务足够长的时间，让你的大脑和身体重新复位。

斯特雷尔指出番茄工作法作为一个有用的起点。这个经典的时间管理策略包括专注工作时段和短暂休息——通常是25分钟专注工作后休息5分钟——尽管他说确切的时间可以调整。“我会从那个开始，然后根据你自己的需要定制，”他说。“但整个目标是让它是有思考驱动的，而不是被轰炸你的各种刺激所驱动的。”

这正是我过去搞错的部分。我一直把离开主要任务的任何时刻都当作休息，即使我仍在给大脑喂更多的噪音。邮件、短信、标题新闻和社交媒体都给了我一些新的东西来反应，而不是一个复位。微休息不同，因为它有边界：你故意停下来，做一些简单的事情，然后在停顿变成绕道之前回来。

微休息给你的大脑和身体一个机会，在它们开始发送越来越戏剧性的警告信号之前恢复。斯特雷尔指向前额皮质——大脑中帮助解决问题和做决定的部分。它依赖于葡萄糖和糖原的能量储备，当你长时间集中注意力时，这些储备可能会开始下降。“进行小小的微休息有帮助，因为它让那些为大脑提供能量和动力的资源、储备得到补充，”他说。这可能表现为一种感觉，正如斯特雷尔所说，“脑子死机”。当我试图硬撑过那种感觉时，我最终会到达这样一个点：我的大脑技术上还在运转，但没什么特别的用处。一切都需要比应该的时间更长。小选择开始变得奇怪地困难。我继续工作，但我变慢了，我的判断也不那么可靠了。

在我进行微休息的那一周，我从休息中回来后并没有突然变得聪明。但我确实获得了一点更多的视角。一个我绕了10分钟的问题看起来更容易处理了。我终于明白了为什么我正在编辑的某个东西感觉“不对劲”。工作并没有变得更容易，确实没有，但它变得更清晰了。斯特雷尔说，当你能在户外进行微休息时，即使只是短暂地出去，这可能尤其如此。“研究表明，在花园里短暂散步可以促进那些神经储备的恢复，”他说。在他关于自然和注意力的研究中，他发现“前额皮质在自然环境中往往会平静下来，不会那么焦躁或活跃。然后，当你回到认知任务时，你的表现会更好。”

这并不意味着每个微休息都必须是穿过植物园的浪漫小漫步。在我的实验中，我走到街角的咖啡店。我在阳台上拉伸。我在公寓楼的庭院里坐着，渴望地看着泳池里变化的水面。而且，毫不奇怪，我的身体喜欢这些小小的释放时刻。长时间专注对生产力来说很棒，但问题是我们中的许多人实际上并没有获得长时间的专注。我们获得的是假的专注：工作一会儿，查邮件，看一眼短信，打开一个标题新闻，然后试图回想我们刚才做到哪里了。斯特雷尔说，这正是结构化休息程序有帮助的原因。当你强迫自己一次完成几个小时的工作时，“真的很想查邮件或社交媒体或新闻推送”，只是为了短暂的分散注意力，他说。但那些小检查并不是无害的。“研究确实表明，我们不太擅长多任务处理，而且我们回到任务状态的能力也不好。”

这就是有计划的微休息和随机中断之间的区别。当我进行有意的微休息时，我通常会带着焕然一新的感觉回来。当我在办公桌前“休息”时查邮件或刷信息流，我回来时会带着三件新事情要考虑，还有一股模糊的感觉：社会正在崩溃。不算是恢复性的。

它比我预期的效果更好，但只有在我把斯特雷尔的建议字面理解之后：从番茄工作法开始，然后定制它。起初，我两方面都不擅长。我设了计时器，然后立刻把它当作一个我不尊重的人的建议。计时器响了，我会想，我写完这句话就停。然后句子变成段落，段落变成邮件，邮件变成我意识到我已经90分钟没动过了，所以我不如继续坚持。通常我的日子就是这样过的。

所以，与其试图遵循一个完美的日程表然后失败，我用了最喜欢的健康技巧：降低期望。我把时间安排当作框架而不是命令，目标是每30到60分钟休息一次短休息，取决于任务而定。有时我离开五分钟。有时我离开两分钟。有时我只是站起来看向窗外，像一个等待前线消息的古典剧中的女人。允许自己这种灵活性对我至关重要。我确实经常感觉到自己在抵抗微休息，当我意识到它要来的时候。每次计时器响，我的第一反应是：绝对不行。我们很忙。但当我真的让自己离开时，工作并没有崩溃。没有截止日期爆炸。没有Teams消息弹出问我为什么放弃了在面无表情的猫面前晃动玩具的神圣职责五秒钟。

到了一周的中途，休息开始感觉不那么具有破坏性，更像是一种节奏：工作、暂停、移动、回来。我没有做得更少；我只是减少了在脑子显然已经离开后假装工作的浪费时间。我感觉更有生产力了吗？是的，虽然不是那种宏大的方式。但我感觉更敏锐了。我在自己滑入假生产力之前抓住了自己。我很少有那种技术上在办公桌前但精神上在迷雾机器中漫游的时刻了。一天结束时我也感觉更好了。不是“准备好跑5公里”那种更好。让我们保持脚踏实地。但更少精疲力竭、更少僵硬、更少感觉被笔记本电脑个人针对了。

最大的错误是把微休息当作额外的生产力时间，因为那样就把休息变成了另一项任务。以下是帮助我正确进行微休息的方法。

我确实认为这个实验改变了我看待工作日的方式。之前，休息感觉是我在足够生产力之后才能获得的东西。现在它们感觉更像是生产力本身的一部分。这并不意味着每天都很完美。我仍然忽略计时器然后意识到一个小时后我还没有进行微休息。我仍然偶尔说服自己“再做一件事”是计划而不是陷阱。我肯定仍然吃午餐时离键盘太近。但当我确实进行微休息时，这一天感觉不那么累人了。不是和笔记本电脑对视八小时，而是眨眼，只是稍微眨一下。而且我确实感觉到了不同。正如斯特雷尔提醒我的，试图“就这么硬撑过去”可能感觉有生产力，但它忽略了我们身体实际需要的东西。“它根本不是我们工作的方式，”他说。“我们是需要休息的生理存在。”

关于专家

在《读者文摘》，我们致力于由具有专业知识和经验的作家生产高质量内容，并咨询相关合格的专家。



我们依赖信誉良好的主要来源，包括政府机构、专业组织、学术机构，以及在适当情况下的作者个人经历。我们验证所有事实和数据，以可靠来源作为支撑，并随着时间推移持续复核以确保其准确性和时效性。了解更多关于我们的团队、撰稿人和编辑政策的信息。这篇题为《我尝试了“微休息”来提高工作效率——意想不到地改变了我的每一天》的文章首次发表于《读者文摘》。
articles.summary:
By 2:43 p.m. on a Monday, I had become one with my chair.

Not in a peaceful, ergonomic way. More in the “has anyone seen her blink lately?” way. I had answered emails, edited copy, opened the same tab three separate times and ignored my water bottle so thoroughly that it may as well have been decorative. My body was technically present, but my brain had started giving me the spinning rainbow wheel.

This is the part of the workday when I usually make one of two bad choices. Either I keep pushing through and call it discipline, or I take what I insist is a “quick break” and somehow end up 45 minutes deep in a Reddit thread, wondering how I’m supposed to reenter normal society, let alone my inbox.

So when I heard about microbreaks—short, planned pauses that are supposed to help you work better by briefly not working—I was skeptical in the way only a person with insane deadlines can be skeptical. Lovely idea. Very cute. But was a three-minute break really going to help when my current options seemed to be “never stop” or “accidentally disappear into my phone”?

Still, the promise was appealing: less stiffness, less brain fog, maybe even better focus. Plus, there’s plenty of research behind the idea. A 2022 review published in PLOS One found that short breaks of 10 minutes or less can help reduce fatigue and improve well-being. And a 2023 study by Columbia University researchers found that five-minute walking breaks every half hour can help offset some of the physical effects of sitting too long.

That was enough to make me try microbreaks for a week. I did not expect a few minutes away from my desk to change my day in any meaningful way. But my usual approach of staying put until my brain gets fuzzy and my shoulders creep up to my ears was clearly not a winning strategy. So it couldn’t hurt. What I found, though, made me rethink my entire approach to work and life.

Get Reader’s Digest’s Read Up newsletter for more life hacks, humor, cleaning, travel, tech and fun facts all week long.

Are we really getting up from our desks less these days?

In a word, yes. Many of us are spending long stretches at our desks without meaningful breaks—and even lunchtime is not safe from the laptop. According to ezCater’s 2024 Lunch Report, 98% of employees recognize that lunch breaks can help job performance and productivity, but only 38% take a break away from their desk every day at lunchtime. Another 2024 survey conducted by Opinium Research for Little Leaf Farms found that 76% of non-remote corporate workers eat lunch at their desks at least half the time.

Desk lunch is one of those habits that feels efficient right up until you are answering emails with a fork in one hand and end up with salad dressing on your keyboard.

But the issue is also bigger than lunch. We sit down, lock in and expect our brains to perform like machines that do not require movement, daylight or the occasional look at something that is not a screen.

David Strayer, PhD, a professor of cognition and neural science at the University of Utah who studies attention, says the problem is not just that sitting all day makes us stiff. It’s that our brains are not built to stay locked onto demanding work for hours without recovery. Over time, performance can start to drop, a pattern researchers call a vigilance decrement. “There’s a lot of literature on this,” Strayer says. “When people had to stay on task for a long period of time, there’d be this initial drop from higher levels of performance, and their performance degraded.”

What are microbreaks, exactly?

Microbreaks are short, intentional pauses during the workday that usually last just a few minutes and no more than 10 minutes.

That sounds almost too small to matter, which is probably why I initially underestimated them. I hear the word break and picture something more substantial: lunch, a nap, a two-week silent retreat where no one can ask me to “circle back.” But microbreaks are more like punctuation marks. They interrupt the workday before it becomes one endless sentence with no commas and a lot of lower back pain. The point is not to cram in another task; it’s to stop doing the main task long enough for your brain and body to reset.

Strayer points to the Pomodoro Technique as a useful starting point. The classic version of this time-management strategy involves focused work periods followed by short breaks—usually 25 minutes of focused work followed by a five-minute break—though he says the exact timing can be adjusted. “I would start with that and then customize it to your own needs,” he says. “But the whole goal of it is to be thoughtfully driven as opposed to just stimulus driven by whatever things bombard you.”

That was the part I’d gotten wrong in the past. I had been treating any moment away from the main task as a break, even if I was still feeding my brain more noise. Emails, texts, headlines and social media all gave me something new to react to, not a reset. A microbreak is different because it has boundaries: You stop on purpose, do something simple and return before the pause turns into a detour.

What do microbreaks do for your brain and body?

Microbreaks give your brain and body a chance to recover before they start sending increasingly dramatic warning signs.

Strayer points to the prefrontal cortex, the part of the brain that helps with problem-solving and decision-making. It relies on energy reserves of glucose and glycogen, and when you concentrate for too long, those reserves can start to dip. “Taking little microbreaks helps because it lets those resources, those reserves that supply and energize the brain, to replenish,” he says.

That can show up as feeling, as Strayer put it, “brain dead.” When I try to push through that feeling, I eventually reach the point where my brain is technically on but not especially useful. Everything takes longer than it should. Small choices start to feel strangely difficult. I keep working, but I’m slower and my judgment is less reliable.

During the week I took microbreaks, I did not come back from them suddenly brilliant. But I did gain a little more perspective. A problem I had been circling for 10 minutes looked more manageable. I finally realized why something I was editing felt “off.” The work did not get easier, exactly, but it got clearer.

Strayer says that may be especially true when your microbreaks get you outside, even briefly. “The research suggests that short walks out into a garden boost those neural reserves,” he says. In his own work on nature and attention, he has found that “the prefrontal cortex tends to settle down and not be so agitated or active in those natural settings. And then, when you come back to a cognitive task, your performance is better.”

That does not mean every microbreak has to be a romantic little stroll through an arboretum. During my experiment, I walked to the coffee shop on the corner. I stretched on my balcony. I sat in my building’s courtyard, staring longingly at the shifting waters of the pool. And, unsurprisingly, my body liked those little moments of release.

Hold on—aren’t long periods of focus supposed to be better for productivity?

Long periods of focus can be great for productivity, but the problem is that many of us are not actually getting long stretches of focus. We are getting fake focus: working for a while, checking email, glancing at a text, opening a headline, then trying to remember where we left off.

Strayer says that is exactly why a structured break routine can help. When you’re forcing yourself to get through hours of work at a time, “it’s really tempting to check your email or your social media or the news feed,” just for a brief distraction, he says. But those little checks are not harmless. “The research really shows we don’t multitask very well, and we don’t come back on task as well.”

That is the difference between a planned microbreak and a random interruption. When I took an intentional microbreak, I usually came back refreshed. When I “took a break” at my desk by checking email or doomscrolling, I came back with three new things to think about and the vague sense that society was crumbling. Not exactly restorative.

So how well did taking microbreaks work for me—really?

It worked better than I expected, but only after I took Strayer’s advice literally: Start with the Pomodoro idea, then customize it.

At first, I was not great at either part. I set a timer and immediately treated it like a suggestion from someone I did not respect. The timer would go off, and I would think, I’ll stop after this sentence. Then the sentence became a paragraph, the paragraph became an email, and the email became me realizing I had not moved in 90 minutes, so I might as well keep pushing. Which is usually how my days go anyway.

So instead of trying and failing to follow a perfect schedule, I used my favorite wellness technique and lowered my expectations. I treated the timing as a framework, not a mandate, aiming for short breaks every 30 to 60 minutes depending on the task. Sometimes I took five minutes away. Sometimes I took two. Sometimes I simply stood up and looked out a window like a woman in a period drama waiting for news from the front. Allowing myself that flexibility was crucial for me.

I did often feel myself resisting the microbreak when I knew it was coming. Every time the timer went off, my first instinct was: Absolutely not. We are busy. But when I actually let myself step away, the work did not fall apart. No deadline burst into flames. No Teams message popped up demanding why I had abandoned my sacred duty to wiggle a toy in front of my impassive cat for five minutes. By halfway through the week, the breaks started to feel less disruptive and more like a rhythm: work, pause, move, return. I was not doing less; I was just wasting less time pretending to work after my brain had clearly left the premises.

Did I feel more productive? Yes, though not in some grand way. I felt sharper, though. I caught myself before I slid into fake productivity. I had fewer stretches where I was technically at my desk but mentally wandering through a fog machine. I also felt better at the end of the day. Not “ready to run a 5K” better. Let’s remain grounded. But less drained, less stiff and less personally victimized by my laptop.

What mistakes should you avoid so you don’t mess up your microbreaks?

The biggest mistake is treating microbreaks like extra time to be productive, because that turns a break into just another task. Here’s what helped me get them right.

  • Don’t turn your break into another work task. Strayer says to do something other than what the primary task is. If you stop writing to answer an email, that may be a task switch, but it is not a break.
  • Don’t let your phone hijack your microbreaks. Social media and news feeds can spark rumination that follows you back to your desk, Strayer warns. Personally, I do not need a five-minute break that ends with me thinking about a stranger’s bad take for the rest of the afternoon.
  • Don’t wait until you’re already fried. Microbreaks worked best when I took them before I hit the wall. Once I was running on fumes, the recovery period had to be longer.
  • Don’t make the timing another thing to stress about. Some tasks need longer focus blocks. Some days are chaos. The point is to build in breaks thoughtfully, not turn your timer into a tiny tyrant.
  • Do use a timer as a reminder. I would love to be the kind of person who simply remembers to take restorative pauses. I am not. A timer helped me notice when I’d been sitting too long, but I treated it like a nudge, not a commandment.
  • Do move if you can. Walking outside worked best for me, but stretching, standing or walking around indoors can make a difference too.

So am I now a microbreak evangelist?

I do think the experiment changed how I look at my workday. Before, breaks felt like something I had to earn after enough productivity. Now they feel more like part of the productivity itself.

That does not mean every day goes perfectly. I still ignore timers and then realize I haven’t taken my microbreak an hour later. I still occasionally convince myself that “just one more thing” is a plan rather than a trap. And I definitely still eat lunch too close to my keyboard.

But when I do take microbreaks, the day feels less exhausting. Instead of getting into a staring contest with my laptop for eight hours, I blink, just a bit. And I really do feel the difference.

As Strayer reminded me, trying to “just slog through it all” may feel productive, but it ignores what our bodies actually need. “It just isn’t the way we work,” he says. “We’re physiological beings that need breaks.”

About the expert

  • David Strayer, PhD, is a professor of cognition and neural science at the University of Utah who studies attention.

Why trust us

At Reader’s Digest, we’re committed to producing high-quality content by writers with expertise and experience in their field in consultation with relevant, qualified experts. We rely on reputable primary sources, including government and professional organizations and academic institutions as well as our writers’ personal experiences where appropriate. We verify all facts and data, back them with credible sourcing and revisit them over time to ensure they remain accurate and up to date. Read more about our team, our contributors and our editorial policies.

  • PLOS One: “‘Give me a break!’ A systematic review and meta-analysis on the efficacy of micro-breaks for increasing well-being and performance”
  • Columbia University Irving Medical Center: “Rx for Prolonged Sitting: A Five-Minute Stroll Every Half Hour”
  • ezCater: “ezCater’s Third Annual Lunch Report Reveals Lunch Breaks Are Losing Ground to Busy Schedules”

articles.title: Is It Really Rude to Not Bring a Gift If an Invitation Says “No Gifts”?
articles.content:
作为一个习惯了短信邀请或“精致”电子请帖的人，我收到一封真正的纸质邀请函时感到非常惊喜，而且确实很精致。就是那种精致程度，让我在衣橱前站了20分钟，想着最好的外出衬衫是否够格。这是一位富豪熟人的生日派对——不是密友，但我希望将来能成为密友——邀请函上写得很清楚：请勿送礼。

好的！太好了！很简单！但派对越接近，我就越焦虑。不带礼物参加一个送礼场合就是感觉不对。万一其他人都带了呢？于是我在路上停在超市停车场，盯着远处看了大约45秒，然后决定买一束鲜花。这感觉是女主人礼物和生日礼物之间的一个很好的折中方案。以防万一。

这是正确的选择。至少一半的客人都带了东西。寿星女例行公事地说：“哦，你不该这样的！”但明显很高兴我们还是带了。派对的其余部分进行得很好，我回家时既隐隐松了口气，又隐隐感到困惑。这里的礼仪规则到底是什么？为什么要求不送礼，但似乎期望的却是送礼？我真的想和那些需要把读心术作为社交技能的人做朋友吗？

原来，这就是“无礼”困境的核心。这是一个大家都想表现得礼貌，结果反而让所有人都感到困惑的礼仪情境。让我们来讨论一下。

人们出于各种真正好的理由在邀请函上写“请勿送礼”。也许他们的孩子是第四个孩子，他们想要的是庆祝婴儿出生的派对，而不是积累更多的婴儿连体衣。或者他们在考虑可能感到经济压力的客人。也许他们想避免看着一个5岁的孩子撕开40份礼物，而大人们站在周围吃蛋糕的混乱场面。我们不都想减少杂物、浪费、塑料或以上所有吗？

对于成年人的派对，尤其是这样，“请勿送礼”通常是一种真诚的尝试，旨在减轻庆祝活动的交易压力。当你庆祝一个重要的生日或举办一个轻松随意的晚宴时，你可能真的只是想让人们来参加并享受这个时刻。礼物可能感觉像是一种入场费，而一个体贴的主人不想让客人感到有义务。

这种“请勿送礼”的趋势已经发展多年，在儿童派对上也变得越来越常见。根据2024年对400多名家长的一项调查，大约八分之一的生日邀请函上写着“请勿送礼”，但仍有大约十分之三的家长还是会带礼物来，平均礼物价值约28美元。这意味着“请勿送礼”的指示更像是一个社会建议，而不是一个硬性规则。你认为这好还是坏很大程度上取决于你是主人还是客人。

这很直接：这是对方的派对，他们告诉你他们想要什么。尊重这一点，从根本上讲，就是良好礼仪的核心。

遵守这一要求也有实际的论据。当一些客人带礼物而另一些没有——因为有些人遵守了指示而有些人没有——你就创造了一种双重标准的情况，遵守规则的人看起来像是吝啬，违反规则的人看起来像是在炫耀。主人然后必须悄悄处理一堆礼物，同时假装没有一堆礼物。这对包括带礼物的人在内的每个人都很尴尬。

送礼物会产生互惠义务。你带了一些东西，所以现在主人觉得需要在你的下一个生日给你一些东西。在足够多的社会关系中重复这个模式足够多次，突然之间每个人都上了一台没有人签过名的礼物跑步机。有时候“请勿送礼”是主人试图帮大家一个忙。

人类自从大约永恒以来就一直在庆祝活动中互赠礼物，这种本能不会因为有人在邀请函底部印了两个字就消失。送礼物是一种爱的语言，有其原因。这是一种出现的方式，一种说“我看到你了，我想尊重这个时刻”的方式。对于许多人来说，在许多文化中，空着手参加庆祝活动会感觉真的不对，而不仅仅是社交上的尴尬。

还有一个问题是你与这个人的特定关系。如果这是你最好的朋友的生日，你们总是交换礼物，“请勿送礼”可能并不意味着你们两个必须停止；它的意思是普通客人不需要送礼。亲密关系有自己的规范，优先于邀请函上的语言。

另一个问题，正如我在那个精致派对上发现的，是没有办法可靠地提前知道其他客人是否也会遵守。你可以是唯一认真对待指示的人。也可能是唯一没有遵守的人。感觉自己在任何一个方向上都收到了错误的备忘录，这本身就是一种特别不舒服的感觉。

好消息是，“请勿送礼”实际上并不意味着“你只能带着微笑出现，希望一切顺利。”有一些中间地带的选项可以让你尊重请求的精神，同时仍然是你这样体贴的人。

一张卡片总是受欢迎的。一张真诚的手写便条不算作礼物，这确实是一个可爱的举动。如果你想附上一些小东西，送到当地餐厅或面包店的礼品卡与包装好的礼物不同，而且主人可以很容易地收起来而不张扬。

鲜花可以工作，但有一个重要的警告。如果你选择这条路（像我一样），带已经插好的，而不是散枝需要主人找到花瓶和剪刀，同时应付12个客人和一轮开胃菜。就像节日派对礼仪一样，目标是要带一些不会在主人试图主持的关键时刻给他们增加更多工作的东西。一瓶葡萄酒或漂亮的巧克力作为“女主人礼物”而不是生日礼物是另一个可靠的选择。这是一种对你被邀请的感谢姿态，而不是你忽视邀请函的声明。

如果你想做更有意义的事情，考虑向对这个重要的人有意义的事业做一点捐赠。有些人真的很喜欢这个；其他人觉得有点 impersonal。了解你的听众。

你绝对不应该做的是带一个大的、包装好的礼物，需要公开打开，让主人在表演惊讶和感激的同时，还要让每个没带礼物的客人感到自己做错了选择。如果你决定要送给一个要求你不要送礼的人一份真正的礼物，那就提前送过去或事后送去。

我想特别谈谈儿童生日派对，因为计算方式不同。对于成年人，你可以权衡你与主人的关系、事件的正式程度和你自己的舒适度。对于儿童的生日庆祝，有一个小孩在中心，他们已经期待这一天好几个月了，并不完全理解为什么大人们一直在为此纠结。父母最了解自己的孩子，所以如果他们说"请勿送礼"，我会认真对待这个要求。

我也亲眼见过这种情况演变成家庭权力斗争——父母试图限制孩子收到的大量物品，而祖父母觉得他们有权用大量物品宠坏孙子。在这种情况下，你绝对应该遵循父母不送礼的指示。

如果你愿意，你仍然可以带一张有趣的卡片。孩子们喜欢收到邮件和被关注。同样，你要避免的是大型礼物——需要生日孩子在其他遵守规则、什么都没带的客人面前停下来打开的东西。这会造成主人试图避免的尴尬。

如果你不同意"请勿送礼"的政策呢？这个派对不是关于你的。但如果你真的无法违背你的文化或本性，你可以悄悄地带一些小东西，直接交给父母让孩子以后再打开。只是不要让它成为一件大事。

何时完全跳过礼物：当小手势合适时：有效的方法：一封真诚的卡片，一张餐厅或当地场所的小礼品卡，作为女主人礼物的葡萄酒或巧克力，已经插在花瓶里的鲜花，或者慈善捐赠。不起作用：需要公开打开的大型包装礼物，任何让主人陷入尴尬境地的东

西，或者完全无视请求并带主人明确要求你不要带的礼物。

"请勿送礼"的困境与我大多数专栏相比有一个有趣的转折。从技术上讲有一个正确的礼仪答案（尊重请求），但现实是如此混乱，灰色地带如此之大，以至于判决是真正模糊的。

我的立场是这样的：当邀请函说"请勿送礼"时不带礼物并不粗鲁。实际上，这是技术上正确且符合礼仪的选择。但如果带着一些小而低调的东西也不是粗鲁的，只要你做得体贴。一张真诚的卡片、一个小消耗品、已经插在花瓶里的鲜花都可以。一份奢华包装的礼物需要认可并让其他客人感觉不好是不可取的。说实话，更粗鲁的举动是让它成为每个人的问题。带礼物或不带——只是不要让它成为"一件大事"。

如果你

是主人？要明白有些人会无视你的请求，而且这通常来自好意。收到你没有要求的东西时最优雅的回应总是"非常感谢。"把礼物放到一边，以后再悄悄打开，并相信你的客人是想庆祝你的。即使他们确实应该读一下邀请函。

与此同时，欢迎加入我在停车场的比喻中，当我们凝视空间并想知道我们是否应该只是留在家里。

有一个你无法停止思考的社交情况？给我们发电子邮件至advice@tmbi.com，或在Instagram上给Charlotte发消息@CharlotteHiltonAndersen。

《读者文摘》已经发表了数百篇礼仪故事，帮助读者在不断变化的世界中驾驭沟通。我们定期涵盖诸如为任何场合发送的最佳信息、并不像看起来那么礼貌的礼貌习惯、电子邮件和短信礼仪、商务礼仪、小费礼仪、旅行礼仪等主题。我们致力于制作由具有专业知识和经验的作家在相关合格专家的咨询下撰写的高质量内容。

我们依靠政府、专业组织和学术机构等信誉良好的主要来源，以及我们作家在适当情况下的个人经验。我们验证所有事实和数据，用可信的来源支持它们，并随着时间的推移重新访问它们以确保它们保持准确和最新。了解更多关于我们团队、我们的贡献者和我们的编辑政策。

articles.summary:
As someone who is used to text invitations or “fancy” evites, I was delighted to get a real paper invitation in the mail that was legit fancy. Like, the kind of fancy where I stood in my closet for 20 minutes wondering if my nicest going-out blouse would cut it. It was a milestone birthday party for a wealthy acquaintance—not a close friend, but someone I was hoping might become one—and the invite was very clear: No gifts, please.

Fine! Great! Easy! Except the closer the party got, the more I spiraled. It just feels wrong to show up to a gift-giving occasion without a gift. What if everyone else brought something? So I pulled into a grocery-store parking lot on the way there, stared into the middle distance for approximately 45 seconds and then decided to get a bouquet of flowers. It felt like a good middle ground between a hostess gift and a birthday gift. Just in case.

It was the right call. At least half the guests had brought something. The birthday girl did the requisite “Oh, you shouldn’t have!” while visibly being delighted that we had. The rest of the party was fine, and I went home feeling vaguely relieved but also vaguely bewildered. What exactly was the etiquette rule here? Why ask for no gifts when it appeared the expectation was gifts? And do I really want to be friends with people who require mind-reading as a social skill?

This, it turns out, is the “no gifts” dilemma in a nutshell. It’s one of those gift-etiquette situations where everyone is trying to be polite and somehow everyone just ends up confused. Let’s discuss.

Get Reader’s Digest’s Read Up newsletter for more etiquette, humor, cleaning, travel, tech and fun facts all week long.

Why people say “no gifts”—and whether they mean it

People put “no gifts” on invitations for all kinds of genuinely good reasons. Maybe their kid is the fourth child and they wanted a baby shower to celebrate, not accumulate more onesies. Or they’re thinking about guests who might feel financial pressure. Perhaps they’re trying to avoid the chaos of watching a 5-year-old tear through 40 presents while the adults stand around eating sheet cake. And don’t we all want to reduce clutter, waste, plastic or all of the above?

For adult parties, especially, “no gifts” is often a sincere attempt to take the transactional pressure off a celebration. When you’re celebrating a big birthday or hosting a low-key dinner, you might genuinely just want people to show up and enjoy themselves. Gifts can feel like a cover charge, and a thoughtful host doesn’t want guests to feel obligated.

The “no gifts” trend has been building for years, and it’s becoming more common at kids’ parties. According to a 2024 poll of more than 400 parents, about 1 in 8 birthday invitations now say “no gifts,” and yet roughly 3 in 10 parents still bring one anyway, with the average gift running around $28. Which means the “no gifts” instruction is more of a social suggestion than a hard rule. Whether you think that’s good or bad probably depends a lot on whether you’re the host or the guest.

The case for following the “no gifts” instruction

It’s pretty straightforward: It’s the person’s party, and they told you what they wanted. Respecting that is, at its core, what good manners are about.

There’s also a practical argument for compliance. When some guests bring gifts and others don’t—because some followed the instructions and some didn’t—you create a two-tier situation where the rule followers look like they skimped and the rule breakers look like they are showing off. The host then has to quietly manage a pile of gifts while pretending there’s no pile of gifts. It’s awkward for everyone, including the people who brought something.

And gift-giving creates reciprocal obligation. You bring something, so now the host feels like they need to get you something for your next birthday. Repeat this pattern enough times across enough social relationships and suddenly everyone is on a gifting treadmill that nobody signed up for. Sometimes “no gifts” is the host trying to do everyone a favor.

The case for bringing something anyway

Humans have been giving one another gifts at celebrations since approximately forever, and that instinct doesn’t disappear just because someone printed two words on the bottom of an invitation. Gift-giving is a love language for a reason. It’s a way of showing up, of saying “I see you and I wanted to honor this moment.” For many people, and across many cultures, arriving at a celebration completely empty-handed feels genuinely wrong, not just socially awkward.

There’s also the matter of your specific relationship to the person. If this is your best friend’s birthday and you always exchange gifts, “no gifts” probably doesn’t mean you two specifically have to stop; it means the general guest population is off the hook. Close relationships carry their own norms that supersede the invitation language.

The other issue, as I discovered at that fancy party, is that there’s no reliable way to know in advance whether other guests are also going to comply. You might be the only person who took the instruction seriously. Or you might be the only one who didn’t. Feeling like you got the memo wrong in either direction is its own special kind of uncomfortable.

The gray areas

The good news is that “no gifts” doesn’t actually have to mean “show up with nothing but your smile and hope for the best.” There are middle-ground options that let you honor the spirit of the request and still be the thoughtful person you are.

A card is always welcome. A sincere, handwritten note doesn’t count as a gift for these purposes, and it’s a genuinely lovely gesture. If you want to tuck in something small, a gift card to a local restaurant or bakery hits differently than a wrapped present and is easy for the host to tuck away without fanfare.

Flowers can work, but there’s an important caveat. If you go this route (as I did), bring them arranged, not as loose stems that require the host to locate a vase and find scissors while juggling 12 guests and a round of appetizers. Like holiday-party etiquette, the goal is to bring something that doesn’t create more work for the host at the critical moment they’re trying to host.

A bottle of wine or nice chocolates framed as a “hostess gift” rather than a birthday gift is another solid option. It’s a gesture of appreciation for having you, not a statement that you ignored the invitation.

If you want to do something more meaningful, consider making a small donation to a charity that matters to the person. Some people genuinely love this; others find it a bit impersonal. Know your audience.

What you should absolutely not do is bring a large, wrapped gift that requires public unwrapping and puts the host in the position of performing surprise and gratitude while making every giftless guest feel like they made the wrong call. If you are determined to give a real gift to someone who asked you not to, send it beforehand or drop it off afterward.

The kid factor

I want to specifically address children’s birthday parties here because the calculus is different. With adults, you can weigh your relationship to the host, the formality of the event, and your own comfort level. With kids’ parties, there’s a small human at the center of it all who has been looking forward to this day for months and does not fully understand why adults keep having feelings about it. The parents understand their child best, so if they say “no gifts,” then I would take that seriously.

I’ve also personally seen this turn into a family power struggle—with the parents trying to limit the massive amount of things that come with children, and grandparents feeling like it is their right to spoil the grandchildren with massive amounts of things. In which case, you should definitely follow the parents’ instructions to not bring a gift.

You can still bring a fun card if you’d like. Kids love getting mail and being seen. Again, what you want to avoid is the large-production gift—something that requires the birthday child to stop and open it in front of other guests who followed the rules and brought nothing. That creates the exact awkwardness the host was trying to prevent.

What if you disagree with the “no gifts” policy? This party isn’t about you. But if you really can’t go against your culture or nature, you are allowed to quietly bring something small and hand it directly to the parents for the child to open later. Just don’t make it a whole thing.

A handy cheat sheet

When to skip the gift entirely:

  • Work events, office parties, professional contexts
  • Large celebrations where you don’t know the guest of honor well
  • Any event where the language is emphatic (“Absolutely no gifts, please!”)
  • When you’re confident others will comply and a gift would be conspicuous

When a small gesture is appropriate:

  • A close friend or family member’s birthday
  • Intimate parties where a hostess gift makes sense regardless
  • Kids’ parties where you want to acknowledge the child
  • When you’d feel genuinely rude arriving without anything

What works: A heartfelt card, a small gift card to a restaurant or local spot, wine or chocolates as a hostess gift, flowers already in a vase, or a charitable donation

What doesn’t work: A large wrapped present that demands public opening, anything that puts the host in an awkward spot, or ignoring the request entirely and bringing the exact thing the host asked you not to

The verdict

The “no gifts” dilemma has an interesting twist compared to most of my columns. Technically there is a correct etiquette answer (honor the request), but the reality is so chaotic and the gray areas are so big that the verdict is genuinely ambiguous.

Here’s where I land: Not bringing a gift when the invitation says “no gifts” is not rude. It is, in fact, the technically correct and etiquette-approved choice. But bringing something small and low-key is also not rude, as long as you do it thoughtfully. A sincere card, a small consumable, flowers that are already in a vase are all fine. A lavishly wrapped present that demands acknowledgment and makes other guests feel bad is not fine, regardless of your intentions. The ruder move, honestly, is making it everyone else’s problem. Bring a gift or don’t—just don’t make it A Thing.

And if you’re the host? Know that some people will ignore your request, and that’s usually coming from a good place. The most gracious response to receiving something you didn’t ask for is always “Thank you so much.” Set the gifts aside, open them quietly later, and trust that your guests were trying to celebrate you. Even if they definitely should have read the invitation.

In the meantime, feel free to join me in the metaphorical parking lot as we stare into space and wonder if we should have just stayed home.

articles.title: Is This the World’s Worst Proposal—Or the Best? Judge the Viral Video for Yourself
articles.content:
我们每个人心中都对求婚场景有着自己的想象——也许是在巴黎埃菲尔铁塔下（女孩子总可以做做梦，对吧？），或者是海边日落时分布满蜡烛的浪漫布置——但现实生活并不总是按照剧本上演。虽然确实没有一种“正确”的求婚方式，但互联网似乎已经准备好为此争论不休。

这是一个信号，让你加入这场讨论。因为一段正在疯传的视频让整个互联网彻底分裂，评论区也发挥了它一贯的作用：对视频的每一秒都进行过度解读。有人称这是最甜蜜、最亲密的求婚时刻。也有人？并不这么认为。

继续阅读，了解为什么这场求婚在互联网上引发如此大的分歧，自己判断一下：这是求婚的赞成票……还是反对票？

@burgerqueen1016 真不敢相信我曾经以为自己的求婚照会很好看 #fyp #engagement #thatsmyfiance ♬ 原声 – Patrick Bateman的耳机

六月初发布的一段TikTok视频，目前播放量已超过83.5万次，让纽约情侣乔·弗罗默和悉尼·伯格的订婚成为网络热点——但不是因为完美的旅行瞬间，而是因为他们在意大利浪漫度假10天后发生的事情。

他没有在国外求婚，而是等到他们回到纽约公寓——就在10小时飞行结束后——当时她穿着运动服，头发扎成发髻，没化妆，完全处于旅行后的舒适放松状态。

他的目标是什么？让她完全措手不及。他真的在误导方面下了很大功夫。弗罗默在接受ABC新闻采访时提到，在意大利期间，他给伯格买了一双鞋作为障眼法，短暂地让她以为戒指可能藏在鞋里，求婚可能就在那时发生。（结果并没有——只是一次非常策略性的鞋子“放料”。）

幕后，弗罗默请了两位亲密朋友帮忙，把他们的公寓布置成完整的求婚模式。空间里摆满了伯格最爱的花——向日葵，还有蜡烛和精心布置的场景，小组实际上排练了好几次才达到完美。

为了让这一刻更加实时化，他们架设了网络摄像头，让家人和朋友可以在线观看弗罗默回家后终于单膝跪地的时刻。

评论区分歧很大，也非常直接。许多观众关注的是时机选择，其中一条热门评论写道：“他把戒指忘在家里了？”——这条评论单独就获得了约19万个赞。还有人对整个情况批评更甚，一位网友写道：“不是说要做扫兴的人，但这真的不好笑，我会真的很伤心。”

也有人为弗罗默的决定辩护，指出“随身携带一枚8000美元的戒指旅行压力很大”。对于一位支持者来说，求婚与地点无关——而是在于其中的心意：“你们去度了一个美好的假期，我打赌你们玩得很开心。然后你回到你们的家（我猜你们是共同居住的），在舒适的环境中接受求婚。他显然深思熟虑过，想要让它私密温馨，这甚至体现了他在你最真实的样子中是多么爱你。”

伯格非常开心。她发布了一段后续视频，其中包含了弗罗默准备求婚的幕后花絮，配文写道：“感谢大家的反馈，但我很喜欢我的求婚，我完全惊呆了！这非常符合我们的风格，很不幸（或很幸运）一个美学的意大利求婚并不适合我们！”

她还分享说，庆祝并没有就此结束。弗罗默在中央公园安排了一场专业摄影拍摄，以更讲究的造型捕捉订婚时刻。

articles.summary:
We all have an idea in our heads of what we want our proposal to look like—maybe in Paris under the Eiffel Tower (a girl can dream, right?), or an over-the-top sunset beach setup with candles everywhere—but real life doesn’t always follow the script. And while there really isn’t one “right” way to propose, the internet seems more than ready to debate otherwise.

This is your sign to join the conversation, because there’s a video going around that’s got the internet completely split, and the comment section is doing what it does best: overanalyzing every second of it. Some people are calling it the sweetest, most intimate proposal moment. Others? Not exactly.

Read on to find out why this proposal has the internet so divided, and decide for yourself: Is it a proposal yay … or a proposal nay?

Get Reader’s Digest’s Read Up newsletter for more news, entertainment, fun facts, humor, cleaning, travel and tech all week long.

What happened in the viral proposal video?

@burgerqueen1016 I can’t believe there was ever a world where I thought I was gonna look pretty at my engagement #fyp #engagement #thatsmyfiance ♬ original sound – Patrick Bateman’s Headphones


A TikTok video posted in early June, now with more than 835,000 views, has made New York couple Joe Fromer and Sydney Burger’s engagement viral—not for a picture-perfect travel moment but for what happened after their 10-day romantic getaway to Italy.

Instead of proposing abroad, he waited until they were back home in their apartment—just after a 10-hour flight—when she was in sweats, had her hair in a bun, wasn’t wearing any makeup and was completely in post-travel comfort mode.

His goal? To completely catch her off guard. And he really committed to the misdirection. In an interview with ABC News, Fromer mentioned that during their time in Italy, he bought Burger a pair of shoes as a red herring, briefly convincing her that a ring might be hidden inside them and a proposal might happen then. (It didn’t—it was just a very strategic shoe drop.)

Behind the scenes, Fromer enlisted two close friends to transform their apartment into full proposal mode. The space was filled with Burger’s favorite flowers, sunflowers, along with candles and a carefully staged setup the group actually practiced multiple times to get right.

To make the moment even more real-time, they set up a webcam so family and friends could watch live as Fromer finally got down on one knee after returning home.

What are people saying?

The comments section has been pretty split, and pretty blunt. Many viewers focused on the timing choice, with one viral comment reading, “He forgot the ring at home?”—a line that picked up around 190,000 likes on its own. Others were more critical of the whole situation, with one person writing, “Not too be a negative Nancy but this really isn’t funny, I’d truly be heartbroken.”

Some defended Fromer’s decision, pointing out how “stressful it is trying to travel with an $8,000 ring in your pocket.” For one supporter, though, the proposal wasn’t about the destination at all—it was about the thought behind it: “You went on a beautiful vacation, I bet you guys had the best time. Then you come back to your home (that I’m assuming you share) where you are comfortable and get proposed to. He clearly thought this out and wanted it to be intimate and [it] even shows how much he loves you in your truest form.”

How did the fiancé respond?

Burger was joyfully surprised. She posted a follow-up video that featured behind-the-scenes clips of Fromer’s preparation for the proposal, captioning it: “THANKS EVERYONE FOR YOUR FEEDBACK BUT I LOVED MY ENGAGEMENT AND I WAS TOTALLY STUNNED!!!! It was perfectly us and unfortunately (or fortunately) an aesthetic Italy proposal just isn’t!”

She also shared that the celebration didn’t end there. Fromer booked a professional photo shoot in Central Park to capture the engagement moment in a more styled setting.
